description: Explains how Requests is installed from PyPI or source, what Python/package metadata controls installation, and how development, test, tox, and documentation setup are wired.
document:
Requests presents ordinary installation as a simple package-manager workflow. The README and the user installation page both direct users to install the library from PyPI with `python -m pip install requests`, and the README states that the supported runtime is Python 3.10 or newer. The installation guide also treats source acquisition as a normal alternative: developers can clone the public GitHub repository or download an archive, then install the checked-out source into site-packages with `python -m pip install .`. That separation is important for easy setup questions: end users need the PyPI command, while source users first obtain the repository and then ask pip to install the local project.

The installable package is described primarily in `pyproject.toml`. It uses setuptools as the build backend, declares the project name and README, records the Apache license text, and marks the version as dynamic from the Requests package itself. The same metadata enforces the Python 3.10-or-newer requirement and declares the runtime dependencies that make Requests work: character set detection, internationalized domain handling, urllib3-based HTTP transport, and the certifi certificate bundle. Optional dependency groups are deliberately small. SOCKS proxy support adds PySocks, the chardet-on-Python-3 option selects chardet as an alternate detector, and the security extra is present but empty.

The legacy setup entry point is intentionally thin. `setup.py` first checks the interpreter version and exits with a clear message if Python is too old; otherwise it delegates to setuptools setup using the project metadata. This means the modern metadata file is the source of truth for packaging details, while the setup script mainly protects users who invoke old-style setup commands under an unsupported interpreter.

The README has one repository-specific cloning warning that is separate from Python packaging. Because Requests history contains a commit timestamp that can trip Git's object checks, the documented clone command may need the `fetch.fsck.badTimezone=ignore` setting. The README shows this setting on the clone command itself and also shows how to put the same value in global Git configuration. The user installation page gives the simpler public clone and tarball commands, so the README is the place that explains the special workaround for users who hit that Git validation problem.

Development setup is centered on installing the project plus test tools. `requirements-dev.txt` installs Requests in editable form with SOCKS support, then adds pytest, coverage tooling, the pinned pytest-httpbin fixture package, httpbin, trustme, and wheel. The Makefile's init target installs this development requirements file. Its test and CI targets run the pytest suite, with CI also writing a JUnit report, while the coverage target runs pytest with Requests coverage settings and produces terminal and XML reports.

Tox provides the broader compatibility matrix. `tox.ini` defines environments for Python 3.10 through 3.14, with a default variant and a variant that enables the chardet option. Each tox environment installs the development requirements, enables the security and SOCKS extras, and runs pytest against the tests unless positional arguments override the target. Documentation setup is separate and minimal: the Makefile builds HTML by running make inside the docs directory, and `docs/requirements.txt` pins Sphinx so Read the Docs can build documentation without unexpected breakage.
